speaker: interviewer
text: With Michael Jackson. And he, you were talking about being loaded onto the train leaving Flossenburg.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, you meant, you meant leaving, leaving Hersbruck. We, since those, those that were able to walk, they marched out. And that were called the death marches. Those that were in the [? rear ?] and could walk-- didn't have injuries-- they marched them out, too. Those that had injuries on their legs, they put on open trains, open carts and trains. We couldn't even work. We were, we were-- I was being carried at that time. I probably didn't weigh more than 50 kilograms. And we were carried onto the train station. We didn't know where we were going, and it was open, open cars. And the cars, they must have had-- carried gravel or something, because there was a lot of gravel on the floor. And we were skin and bone. We couldn't sit down on the, on the gravel. They gave us a blanket. We were in shirts and undershirts. And one shoe I had, and the other one was, was bandaged. And the guys, when they were carrying us, we were handed one loaf of bread to go on the train. And when we got on the train, I, there was one from our [INAUDIBLE] that used to be with us. And I, we got together. I told him, let's try to find a corner and, and clean up a little bit the gravel. And we'll, we'll put the two blankets around us so we should be warmer. And that's how they loaded up the train. And that was maybe 50, 60 people that could, that couldn't walk in those open trains. I think they left that same, that same day. And it traveled through the nigh. And it, it stopped next, next, in next station in, in the morning. I don't remember where it was. I didn't know where we were going. But it, it, it didn't snow or rain. How-- next to us was, was a, a Russian, a haftling also, also something wrong with his feet. But it turned out that he was, on top of it, he was blind. And was, he must have had fever. He was crying all night, all night, long to water, water, water, water.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when we, we stopped at one station, I remember, yelled out to some people in them to please give us some water. Somebody, somebody was, was kind enough. And they brought a bottle water to give this guy to drink.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, the SS would also travel in the same train. But they would travel in normal coaches, not in open car. They were in, in that train. I remember there was an air raid. And, and we, we stayed in that place during the day. I think the Americans would fly during the day. The British would fly at night. And the sirens would be howling. But we, we weren't afraid that we were going to be bombarded. It, it was in such a situation that we didn't care what happened anymore. Anyway, that air raid-- I remember talking. There was next to me, a, a, Czech a Czech officer. And, and he, he said he would bet that it's not going to take more than two weeks the way, the way things looked. So we tried to encourage ourselves. It's not going to take long. Anyway, the next day-- it took three or four days till we got to Dachau. The next day, half of the people that were in our train were dead. It started snowing. And it was very cold, and, and, and people that either they had fever or whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when we stopped at one, at one station, the, the, the German SS ordered the Ukrainian SS-- they had the dark uniforms, and the other had the green uniforms-- they ordered them to take the dead people out, because they didn't want the local people to see or something. I don't know why.

speaker: interviewee
text: When they started taking the dead people out from, from our, our wagon, we asked them to leave the, to leave the blankets. And they did. They did leave the blankets. So by the second, the third day, we had, each one had plenty of blankets. So we were able to, to cover ourselves and protect ourselves from the, from the freezing cold. And the third or fourth day, we arrived in Dachau. We didn't know it was Dachau. We didn't know what it was. But when haftlings that were send out from the camp to help us unload the dead and the living, we asked them, where are we, so that then we found out that we are in Dachau. And I remember they were taking off, they were taking off the, the dead and putting on, on, on, on one cart and then taking off the living. They were carrying inside and put them on the ground there. They had to-- you know, the Germans are very meticulous. And they had to account for how many dead and how many alive. But it, if it would matter to-- anyway. Anyway, I remember the two guys spoke in Polish. And one said to the other in Polish, he says, [SPEAKING POLISH]. They put them on, on the dead cart. And the one said to him, he is still alive. He is moving. He's still alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: The other guy, SS, he says [SPEAKING POLISH]. No matter, never mind, he said. He is going to die anyway, so what's the, what's the difference? That's, that's how life was cheap in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, I am going to make it short. We had to go, we had to go to a new [? enclosure, ?] you know, to take, to take a shower. And I, and I couldn't walk. And I, I collapsed in that shower. And I don't know what happened. I found myself the next day, in, in a [INAUDIBLE] in Dachau on the third bunk on top. Find, found myself there. Next to me was a German priest by the name of Mertz. I, I shall never forget this man. He was a very kind and gentle man. He was a political prisoner. He was in Dachau already for six or seven years. And, and because he was resisting the Nazism and Hitler, they put him. But he must have had-- I, I, I can't imagine-- he must have had, after six, seven years, his condition was 10 times better than mine. He must have had family. He did have family that must have, must have had support that they maybe sent him packages or something that they, that he was able to, to, to endure such. I remember the French were getting some packages, Red Cross packages, and they would sell. They would sell. They had cigarettes in these packages. And this guy would give up his ration, a little slice of bread that he was get, to get a cigarette.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I used to tell this guy, I said, Mertz, [? [SPEAKING GERMAN]. ?] You're crazy. What are you doing? You're giving away-- and he, he would answer to me. He said, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. He says, you don't know how to shovel coal. You don't know how to hunger, as though you have to learn how to hunger. It's a trait. If you learn it, you know how to, how to be hungry. That's-- you don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: He would, he would give away his, his-- take a couple puffs, turn it off, cover himself up, up with over the head with his blanket and go to, go to sleep for another four hours till he would be awake again, when, when in the evening, he would get a little, a little soup or something, whatever they would get, or a little [? margarine. ?] Anyway, but he, he was very smart. He saved my-- I think he saved my, my life two days before liberation. This wasn't heard of-- two days before liberation, I also had an operation in, in, in Dachau. In the Dachau-- I'll get back to this-- in the Dachau, I, I developed a boil in the back laying, not, laying in, in, in, in skin and bone when they took me in. But in Dachau, when they took me in to the hospital to the [INAUDIBLE], it was, it was a real hospital. This was a show place, because Dachau was one of the first concentration camps. The Germans started it in 1934, '35. They wanted to show the world that this is, this is a modern hospital. And they took care. You know, it was a show place.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it really was. We came into this hospital. They had anesthesia over there. They put you to sleep. Anyway, where was I? You were having your operation.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had, I had the operation in there. Yeah. Two days before liberation, rumors go fly around that the Germans are mining the, the, the, the camp. And if-- that they are going to blow it up. And there are 38,000 inmates in there. The camp was built to hold only 3,000, 4000, or 5,000. 38,000 were jammed in there by then. So you, you, you can imagine what was going on in our, in our own minds. We were so close and maybe so far, so close to liberation. And maybe in one second, they are blow the whole camp up, and we won't see it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, two days before liberation, or three days, they started giving out for the people in the [INAUDIBLE], a whole box of Ovaltine. You know what Ovaltine is? It's dried eggs, sugar, honey, milk, dried milk. It's very, very rich in, in calories and nourishing-- and a whole box.

speaker: interviewee
text: When they-- and, and, and we couldn't believe. This wasn't heard of. What happened was that they had this was sent through the Red Cross through Switzerland. And the thousands of them they had in the, in the, in the magazines. And the Americans were already so close. They were afraid they will come in and find all that food while they see that thousands of people are dying here out of hungry. So they decided they, they give it out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this guy, Mertz, he was smart enough. And he told me, don't you dare. He said, just take your spoon and give it a lick a little bit. Don't you dare to eat too much. Because you know what happened? A lot of people, they started eating it. And they actually killed themselves. They died from, from, from, from over-eating that such rich food. And, and, and their, their bodies couldn't, couldn't, couldn't take it. So anyway, on April the 29th, at quarter to five-- what brigade was it? The American Third Armored Brigade reached the camp of Dachau. And some of the, some of the-- the camp commander disappeared. The camp leader, the commander, disappeared.

speaker: interviewee
text: But some of the soldiers were still on the towers, on the electric towers. But they were gunned down by, by, by the Americans. The tanks came in. I was so sick that I couldn't see it. But all I could hear is the tremendous jubilation that was going on, in the, on the appellplatz. It was-- we couldn't believe, We couldn't believe ourselves. But what happened was that that night, the Germans attacked again. They wanted to retake the camp. And some bombs fell. And believe it or not, a few people-- inmates-- got killed on the, on the last day, day. And this was the 29th of April, 1945. The war lasted another eight days. We didn't know that Roosevelt was dead. We didn't know that Hitler was dead. We didn't know what was going on. We were completely-- that's the end of the story. I, I, I skipped the story about my brother, Tsvi, in Hersbruck where I found out, because I always cry about this, this story. I have written it. And I, and I, I can't, I can't go, go--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet your wife, though?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. [SIGHS] Well, when I was liberated in Dachau, the Americans came in. And-- are you still taping?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Americans came in. And I have to, I have to tell this. They, they brought in, brought in from the city of Dachau-- they brought in from the local population, beds. And some of the big blocks that, that were open, they cleared out. The, the, the chicken coops, they cleared it out. And they had barracks where the German SS was. They were clear barracks. They cleared that up, and they put in beds-- in huge rooms, beds. And they started to take out the sick and, and, and tried to, to select them out and see what they can do with them. And don't forget, the war was still going on a few days, a week.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they had-- the military, the military had the, the portable x-ray machines. And they were x-raying people in, in the beds. And, and they were starting to take, to take stock.

speaker: interviewee
text: I left Dachau the ninth of June, to the sanatorium Gauting. And the first week or two, there were 200 people still dying daily in Dachau, dying from, from, from, from, from the diseases. When I left in June the ninth, which was already exactly a month after the war, there were still 50 people dying every day. And we, in that-- we talked about, about experiments-- in that room where I was, there were two people from Yugoslavia that they conducted experiments on them to know how, how much freezing water the human body is capable of standing. And there, those two people were like one raw, raw piece of flesh. They, both of them died a few, a few days after, after. That was already after liberation. That's the only thing that I came, came across with, with medical.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then how did you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I should say-- yeah. I started saying. I keep forgetting. I weighed 67 pounds. I was sick, sick, skin and bone. I wasn't able to turn around. That's how weak I was. And when the, when the American doctor first came, and I was reading his mind. I was looking at him. And he had a helper. He didn't speak English, but he had a helper that, that spoke German. And I was reading his mind. And he, and I saw that he looked at me, and he gave up. He gave up on me. And he said something in, in English to this, this here. And he start walking away from me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when he starts walking away, I started to yell. And I started to cry. And I told, doctor, doctor, come back. Come back, I said. Now that I lived to see the end of Hitler, the end of the war, now you're giving up on me? Why are you going away from me?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I, I must have, I must have been sweated up. And he came back. And he kind of wiped my, my forehead. And he looked at me again. And then he looked on the bed. Already, we had a card the nationality. The nationality is Czech nationality, because that's, that, that's what I had registered that time. And he said to this guy to tell me that somebody is going to come in the afternoon with medicine for me. And sure enough, around 2:00, 3:00 o'clock in the afternoon, another guy and a, and a, and a nurse came in with a, with a one of those contraptions. And they hooked up a blood transfusion-- blood plasma-- and into my vein. And they had it, they had it made so that it goes very, very slowly. He wasn't sure. He told me that that helper of his told, told me that he didn't believe that, that he can-- that I would, I would be capable of. To make the story short, I had six blood transfusions, two one in a row, and three in every second, third day. And, and, and so, and all of a sudden, I started, started to feel alive. And I think this was this Major [? Killinger ?] that I looked for when I spoke to this, to this Colonel Walter J. Fellenz that liberated the camp. And, and he said that, that I should contact the Pentagon. My son dead. We couldn't, couldn't locate. Maybe it's not the-- maybe I have the name wrong. And, and so we were there. And, and four, five weeks later, on June the nine, I was sent into a regular hospital that was one of the finest hospitals the Germans had built for the Air Force, the German Air Force. It was called the Luftwaffen Hospital. And over there, I had, for more than one year's time, more than one year's time, I had over a 102 fever every day. I had pleurisy. They were pumping gallons of, gallons of water from my, my lungs out. But I was 25 years old then. And I was-- in spite of all the, all the, the, the illness and the, and the, and the fever-- I was able in the morning breakfast, to have a good breakfast. And I kept on gaining. Almost each week, I gained a kilogram, two kilogram, which was two to three to four pounds a week. And a year later, I regained my weight to 80, to 80 kilograms. At that time, that was my normal weight. And, in 19-- so I stayed in this, in this hospital till 1947. In 1947, I was released from the hospital and sent to Geretsried, where I, where I got a job with the UNRRA, the UNRRA, United Nations Refugee Relief organization. The UNRRA later was changed to, to the IRO, International Refugee Organization. And I worked for them in the camp, in the DP camp, Geretsried, until, until the last-- until I decided. Yeah, in 1948, '47, my wife came as an-- not an inmate-- as a, came to, to, to the camp. I was already at that time the camp leader. And she got, got employment in our, in the office. And that's how, how we met in, in 1948. In 1948, we got married. And in 1948-- for that alone, it was worthwhile to, to survive.
